theorem_statement: If every finite subgraph of an infinite graph \(G\) can be colored using at most \(k\) colors, then the entire graph \(G\) is also \(k\)-colorable.
raw_proof_text:
The proof utilizes the Tychonoff Product Theorem from topology. Let \(V\) be the set of vertices of the infinite graph \(G\) and \(K = \{1, 2, \dots, k\}\) be the set of colors. The space of all possible colorings is the product space \(X = K^V\).

By Tychonoff's Theorem, since each \(K\) is finite and thus compact, the product space \(X\) is compact in the product topology. For every finite subgraph \(F \subseteq G\), let \(C_F\) be the set of all colorings \(f \in X\) that are valid \(k\)-colorings when restricted to \(F\).

For an edge \(e = \{u, v\}\) in \(F\), the condition for a valid coloring is \(f(u) \neq f(v)\). Each such condition defines a closed set in the product topology. Since \(F\) is finite, \(C_F\) is the intersection of a finite number of closed sets and is therefore closed in \(X\).

By hypothesis, every finite subgraph is \(k\)-colorable, so \(C_F \neq \emptyset\). Given any finite collection of finite subgraphs \(F_1, \dots, F_m\), their union is also a finite subgraph, and any valid coloring of the union restricted to the components is a valid coloring for each \(F_i\). Thus, the family of closed sets \(\{C_F\}\) possesses the finite intersection property.

In a compact space, a collection of closed sets with the finite intersection property must have a non-empty total intersection. Thus, there exists a coloring \(f^* \in \bigcap C_F\). Because every edge in the infinite graph is contained in some finite subgraph, \(f^*\) is a valid \(k\)-coloring for the entire graph \(G\).
